preFix.title: Lagging the header tank and pipes after a loft insulation top-up
preFix.subtitle: Protecting cold-water storage and feed pipes that are now above the insulation layer
preFix.excerpt: When loft insulation is topped up to floor level, any header tank and feed pipes that were previously sitting in a warm loft become exposed to near-outdoor temperatures. Lagging the tank and pipes before winter prevents burst pipes and protects water quality. This is the procedure for a standard 25-litre to 230-litre cold-water storage tank.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) guidance on loft tanks. Energy Saving Trust loft insulation guidance on tank lagging. CIBSE guide on domestic cold-water storage.

content.text: Before the 2000s, UK houses with gravity-fed water systems had a
header tank

in the loft. When the loft had 100 mm or less of insulation, the tank sat in a space that was warmish from the house heat rising through the ceiling. When insulation is topped up to 270 to 300 mm and the insulation layer sits at floor level, the tank is now in cold air with no warmth rising from below. In a UK winter this means risk of freezing, burst pipes, and water damage. The fix is straightforward: lag the tank and all pipes in the loft before the first cold spell of the year.
Materials
Purpose-made tank jacket (pre-cut foam or glass-fibre segments with drawstring): these come in standard tank sizes (70 L, 115 L, 230 L).
Pipe lagging: 25 mm foam pipe insulation sleeves, split lengthways, in sizes matching the pipe diameter (typically 15 mm and 22 mm for feed pipes in a domestic loft).

Insulating the lid: cut a piece of 50 mm mineral wool or PIR board to fit the lid of the tank. The lid sits loosely on top and is not sealed; sealing it would create an airless environment and risk bacterial growth in standing water.
The tank jacket

Drain the tank if the jacket has to slide over the bottom: close the mains stopcock, then open a cold tap to drain the feed. Most jackets wrap around the tank sides and clip under the lid without draining.
Fit the jacket segments around the sides of the tank. A standard 230 L round tank takes four or six segments depending on the kit. Clip the drawstring to hold segments against the tank sides.

Do not insulate the base of the tank if the tank sits directly on the loft insulation. The small amount of warmth rising through the boards into the tank prevents freezing at the bottom. If the tank is on a raised platform, wrap the underside.
Lay the lid-insulation piece loosely on top of the existing lid. Do not tape or seal it down.
Pipe lagging

Work from the tank outward to the point where the pipe disappears through the ceiling into the warmer house space. Use pre-slit foam sleeves, opening them over the pipe and taping the slit closed with pipe-insulation tape or self-adhesive tape.
At bends: cut two 45-degree mitres in the foam, butt them together around the bend, and tape. Pre-made 90-degree elbow pieces are also available; these are faster for right-angle bends.
Cold feed pipe to the hot cylinder: this is the pipe most at risk because it carries no water when demand is low. Lag along its full run in the loft.

The overflow pipe: this passes through the loft and exits through the eaves. If the pipe is empty (no overflow condition), it acts as a cold-air path. Insulate it along the loft run and ensure the external end has a manufacturer's screened cap to keep insects out.
Modern combination boilers

Houses with a combination boiler have no header tank and no loft cold-water storage. There are no pipes in the loft other than any remaining from a decommissioned gravity system. If a header tank is present but the house now has a combi, the tank is redundant. It should be drained, the valves closed, and the redundant pipes removed or capped. A redundant tank sitting full of stagnant water is a Legionella risk.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: tank wrapped on all sides and top but not sealed, all exposed pipes lagged along their full loft run to where they exit through the ceiling, overflow pipe lagged and capped at the eaves end. No frost damage through the winter.

Done badly: tank unwrapped, feed pipes bare, first hard frost causes a burst at the tank base where water sits still. A burst loft pipe can release the full tank volume (up to 230 litres) into the ceiling before the damage is noticed.
